key_insight: A fully differentiable, GPU-accelerated FEM pipeline can simulate finite-strain anisotropic plasticity and recover constitutive parameters through fast, reliable gradients.
engineering_relevance: This makes inverse material characterization practical for high-dimensional, data-rich solid mechanics problems, reducing reliance on manual derivations and slow finite-difference sensitivity analysis.
method: JAX-based finite element framework with automatic differentiation through constitutive update and solver steps
GPU parallelization of elemental evaluation, sparse assembly, and sparse linear solve
Inverse identification using full-field displacement data from topology-optimized heterogeneous specimens
evidence_signal: On a 3-million-DOF forward problem, the method reached up to 9.4× speed-up on a single NVIDIA H100 versus a 24-core CPU Abaqus baseline and accurately recovered anisotropic yield and hardening parameters.
watch_item: Performance and robustness may depend on GPU memory, sparse-solver scalability, and careful treatment of complex constitutive models in differentiable implementations.
paper_title: A Differentiable GPU-Accelerated Finite Element Framework for Inverse Characterization of Finite-Strain Anisotropic Plasticity
